passages.explanation: After Jesus is born in Bethlehem during the reign of King Herod, wise men from the east arrive in Jerusalem asking: where is the one born king of the Jews? We saw his star in the east and have come to worship him. Herod is disturbed — and all Jerusalem with him. He assembles the chief priests and scribes and asks where the Messiah is to be born. They quote Micah 5:2: Bethlehem of Judea. The religious leaders know the text perfectly but don't act on it. The pagan astrologers travel thousands of miles on a hint; the theologians won't walk six miles on a certainty.

Herod secretly summons the wise men, finds out exactly when the star appeared, and sends them to Bethlehem: go search carefully for the child, and when you find him, report back so I can worship him too. The star leads them to the house where the child is. They see Jesus with Mary, fall down and worship, and open their treasures — gold, frankincense, and myrrh. Then, warned in a dream not to go back to Herod, they return home by another route. The gifts carry symbolic weight — gold for royalty, frankincense for divinity, myrrh for burial. From the start, this king's story points toward the cross.

After the wise men leave, an angel appears to Joseph in a dream: get up, take the child and his mother, and flee to Egypt. Stay there until I tell you, because Herod is going to search for the child to destroy him. Joseph gets up that night and leaves. They stay in Egypt until Herod's death. Matthew sees another fulfilment: out of Egypt I called my son, from Hosea 11:1. Jesus recapitulates Israel's story — like Israel, he goes down to Egypt and is called back out. He's living the nation's history in miniature.

When Herod realises the wise men have outsmarted him, he's furious. He orders the killing of all boys in Bethlehem and the surrounding area who are two years old and under, based on the time he'd learned from the wise men. Matthew connects this to Jeremiah 31:15 — Rachel weeping for her children, refusing to be comforted because they are no more. The Messiah's arrival doesn't prevent suffering; it enters into it. The shadow of state violence falls across the story from the very beginning.

After Herod dies, an angel appears again to Joseph in Egypt: get up, take the child and his mother, and go to the land of Israel, because those who sought the child's life are dead. Joseph obeys, but when he hears that Archelaus is reigning in Judea in place of his father Herod, he's afraid to go there. Warned in a dream, he withdraws to the region of Galilee and settles in a town called Nazareth. Matthew notes this fulfils what was spoken through the prophets: he will be called a Nazarene. Every move the family makes is guided by divine warning and shaped by political danger. Providence doesn't eliminate the threat — it navigates through it.
